name: Atrial Fibrillation
identifyBy: Irregularly irregular rhythm - no two RR intervals are equal
Absent P waves - replaced by fibrillatory baseline (f waves)
Fibrillatory baseline: rapid irregular undulations 350–600/min - best seen in V1
Ventricular rate depends on AV node conduction - typically 100–170 bpm (uncontrolled)
Narrow QRS unless aberrant conduction (LBBB/RBBB) or pre-excitation (WPW)

measurements.pWaves: Absent - replaced by fibrillatory f waves

management: Rate control (preferred for most): Metoprolol, Diltiazem, or Digoxin
Consider rhythm control (cardioversion/antiarrhythmics) for new-onset <48h with symptoms
Anticoagulation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score ≥2 in men / ≥3 in women - DOAC preferred
Pre-cardioversion: if AF >48h or unknown - anticoagulate 3 weeks OR TOE to exclude LAA thrombus
Urgent DC cardioversion if haemodynamically compromised
pearls: AF is the most common sustained arrhythmia - affects 1–2% of the general population
Lone AF in the young: often vagally mediated (post-exercise, post-meal, alcohol)
CHA₂DS₂-VASc score: Congestive HF, Hypertension, Age ≥75 (x2), Diabetes, Stroke/TIA (x2), Vascular disease, Age 65–74, Sex female
Rate vs rhythm control: RACE and AFFIRM trials showed similar outcomes - rate control is simpler
Cardioversion without anticoagulation: risk of stroke if LAA clot dislodges - always anticoagulate first unless emergency
differentials: Sinus arrhythmia (P waves present, same morphology)
Pre-excited AF (wide complex, very fast - see separate entry)

qaNotes: Irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm with fibrillatory baseline and no organised P waves.
